title: Temporal hopping dynamics in exciton-polariton condensation
authors: Elena Rozas, Wojciech Bukalski, Yannik Brune, Adbhut Gupta, Kirk Baldwin, Loren N. Pfeiffer, Hassan Alnatah, Jonathan Beaumariage, David W. Snoke, Paolo Comaron, Marzena H. Szymanska, Marc Aßmann
abstract: Polariton condensates provide a versatile platform for exploring non-equilibrium phase transitions and collective phenomena in open quantum systems. Near the condensation threshold, these systems are particularly sensitive to fluctuations and instabilities, which can strongly influence the condensate formation. Using optical trapping and homodyne detection, we directly access the photon statistics and second-order correlation function $g^{(2)}(0)$ of the condensate. We show that polariton condensation near the threshold is not a purely static transition, but instead undergoes a dynamical regime characterized by stochastic hopping between condensed and non-condensed states. These intermittent dynamics are accompanied by a gradual reduction of $g^{(2)}(0)$ towards unity, revealing the progressive build-up of coherence even in the presence of strong temporal fluctuations. Numerical simulations, based on a stochastic Truncated Wigner description of the driven-dissipative polariton field, reproduce these dynamics and capture the essential role of noise and reservoir interactions. This work demonstrates that the observed temporal hopping is an intrinsic feature of polariton condensation, providing a dynamical perspective that goes beyond static descriptions of the condensation phase transition.
summary: This paper explores the transition to polariton condensation, revealing that the process is not a static phase transition but involves a period of stochastic hopping between states. By using homodyne detection and TWA simulations, the authors show that the system fluctuates wildly near the threshold while simultaneously building up phase coherence. This demonstrates that the observed instabilities are an intrinsic feature of the driven-dissipative nature of polaritons. The work challenges the idea that a stable, stationary state is required for the emergence of coherence.
main_problem: Investigating the temporal dynamics and stability of exciton-polariton condensation near the threshold, specifically whether the observed instabilities are intrinsic to the driven-dissipative nature of the system.
main_result: The study discovers a dynamical regime of stochastic 'hopping' between condensed and non-condensed states near the threshold, demonstrating that coherence (reduction of g2(0) toward 1) can emerge even during these intense temporal fluctuations.
method: Experimental time-resolved homodyne detection and optical trapping are combined with numerical simulations using the stochastic Truncated Wigner Approximation (TWA).
model_or_system: Exciton-polariton condensates in a high-Q GaAs microcavity, modeled via a stochastic partial differential equation framework that couples the polariton field to an excitonic reservoir.
key_observables: Second-order correlation function g2(0), photon number, linewidth, and the average image difference (Id) for detecting temporal fluctuations.
important_parameters: Pump power relative to threshold (P/Pth), specifically identifying an instability window near 1.0 Pth, and the presence of a reservoir-driven driven-dissipative regime.
main_assumptions: The Truncated Wigner Approximation is used, which requires commutator corrections at low densities to remain physically accurate; simulations use a larger pumping ring than the experiment for computational stability.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows occupancy, linewidth, and image difference vs pump power; Fig 2 compares experimental and numerical time-resolved photon numbers; Fig 3 compares experimental and numerical evolution of g2(0).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of threshold dynamics, presents experimental observations of hopping and coherence buildup, validates these with TWA simulations, and concludes with the implications for driven-dissipative systems.
why_interesting: It provides a deep look into non-equilibrium phase transitions and how quantum coherence can be established in a system characterized by strong fluctuations and stochastic switching, which is central to open quantum systems research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Threshold behavior and instability of the polariton condensate. (a) Measured polariton occupancy (black) and its corresponding PL linewidth (purple) as a function of the normalized pump power. The solid lines serve as guides to the eye. Empty diamonds highlight the pump powers at which detailed measurements are performed: 0.95Pth,1.00Pth,1.06Pth,1.10Pth,1.22Pth and 1.35Pth. Two insets show the real-space image of the uncondensed polaritons (left) and the trapped polariton condensate (right). (b) Average image difference Id, as defined in Eq. (1), around the threshold area. The gray shaded area indicates the range of powers where instabilities of the polariton condensate are...

Fig. 2: Time-resolved photon number Experimental (a-f) and numerically simulated (g-l) photon numbers with increasing pump power. Below threshold (a), no measurable signal is detected due to the strong blueshift of the emission, which prevents the spectral overlap with the local oscillator. Panels (b) and (c) are magnified by a factor of three for clarity.

Fig. 3: Time-resolved second-order correlation function g(2)(0). Experimental (a-f) and numerical (g-l) g(2)(0) with increasing pump power. Due to the mismatch between signal and LO at 0.95 Pth (a), no signal is detected and therefore, g(2)(0) results in random values.
